name: Clarissa's house, Westminster
certaintyNote: Woolf never gives an address. The Dalloways live in Westminster within earshot of Big Ben; scholars place the house around Dean's Yard. Sited here as a considered guess, not a fact.
quoteAnchor: Mrs. Dalloway said she would buy the flowers herself.
passage: Mrs. Dalloway said she would buy the flowers herself. For Lucy had her work cut out for her. The doors would be taken off their hinges; Rumpelmayer's men were coming. And then, thought Clarissa Dalloway, what a morning—fresh as if issued to children on a beach.
note: The first line of the novel, and the reason for the whole day: she is going out to buy flowers for a party. Everything that follows hangs off this errand.

name: Big Ben
quoteAnchor: First a warning, musical; then the hour, irrevocable.
passage: There! Out it boomed. First a warning, musical; then the hour, irrevocable. The leaden circles dissolved in the air.
note: The novel's metronome. Big Ben strikes eleven times across the book, and each strike is the hinge Woolf uses to cut from one character to another. 'The leaden circles dissolved in the air' recurs like a refrain — the same sound falling on people who will never meet.

name: Victoria Street
passage: Such fools we are, she thought, crossing Victoria Street. For Heaven only knows why one loves it so, how one sees it so, making it up, building it round one, tumbling it, creating it every moment afresh.
note: Named directly in the text. She is walking north-west, from Westminster towards the parks and the shops.

name: St James's Park
quoteAnchor: She had reached the Park gates
passage: She had reached the Park gates. She stood for a moment, looking at the omnibuses in Piccadilly.
note: The threshold of the walk proper. From here the day opens out.

name: Hatchards, 187 Piccadilly
quoteAnchor: as she looked into Hatchards' shop window
passage: But what was she dreaming as she looked into Hatchards' shop window? What was she trying to recover? What image of white dawn in the country, as she read in the book spread open: Fear no more the heat o' the sun / Nor the furious winter's rages.
note: The bookshop is real, it is named in the text, and it is still at 187 Piccadilly today — one of the very few places in the novel you can stand in front of and see essentially what Clarissa saw. The lines in the window are from Shakespeare's Cymbeline, and they become the novel's private refrain: Clarissa returns to them, and so does Septimus.

sources.ref: Hatchards has traded at 187 Piccadilly since 1797

name: Bond Street — Mulberry's, the florist
certaintyNote: Bond Street is explicit. Mulberry's, the florist, is Woolf's invention — there is no such shop. Sited mid-street as a representative point, not a real address.
passage: Bond Street fascinated her; Bond Street early in the morning in the season; its flags flying; its shops; no splash; no glitter; one roll of tweed in the shop where her father had bought his suits for fifty years; a few pearls; salmon on an iceblock.
note: The destination of the errand. While she is in the flower shop a motor car backfires in the street outside — and that bang is the pivot on which the novel turns from Clarissa to Septimus. The two never meet; the noise is the only thing they share.

name: Regent's Park — the Broad Walk
quoteAnchor: Septimus Warren Smith, who found himself unable to pass
passage: Septimus Warren Smith, who found himself unable to pass, heard him. Septimus Warren Smith, aged about thirty, pale-faced, beak-nosed, wearing brown shoes and a shabby overcoat, with hazel eyes which had that look of apprehension in them which makes complete strangers apprehensive too.
note: The novel's second life begins here. Septimus is a shell-shocked veteran of the Great War, sitting in the park with his wife Rezia while an aeroplane writes letters in the sky above them. He is having a very different morning in the same city, under the same clocks.

name: Westminster — Peter Walsh leaves
certaintyNote: He is leaving Clarissa's house, whose location is itself inferred.
quoteAnchor: The sound of Big Ben striking the half-hour struck out between them
passage: The sound of Big Ben striking the half-hour struck out between them with extraordinary vigour, as if a young man, strong, indifferent, inconsiderate, were swinging dumbbells this way and that.
note: Peter Walsh — who loved Clarissa thirty years ago and still does — has just called on her unannounced and been shown out. The half-hour strikes literally between them as he goes.

name: Whitehall and Trafalgar Square
passage: As he sat smiling at the dead man in the grey suit the quarter struck—the quarter to twelve.
note: Peter walks north through Whitehall and into Trafalgar Square, following a young woman he will never speak to, inventing a life for her. The quarter-hour catches him mid-fantasy.

name: Westminster — the green dress
quoteAnchor: twelve o'clock struck as Clarissa Dalloway laid her green dress on her bed
passage: …and died up there among the seagulls—twelve o'clock struck as Clarissa Dalloway laid her green dress on her bed, and the Warren Smiths walked down Harley Street.
note: Read this stop and the next one together — they are the same sentence, and the same instant. Woolf uses the stroke of twelve to hold two places in one moment: a woman laying out a dress for her party, and a man walking to the doctor who will destroy him. This is what the book does that a map of pins cannot.

name: Harley Street — the appointment
quoteAnchor: and the Warren Smiths walked down Harley Street
passage: …twelve o'clock struck as Clarissa Dalloway laid her green dress on her bed, and the Warren Smiths walked down Harley Street. Twelve was the hour of their appointment. Probably, Rezia thought, that was Sir William Bradshaw's house with the grey motorcar in front of it. The leaden circles dissolved in the air.
note: The same stroke of twelve, two and a half miles north. Sir William Bradshaw is the eminent psychiatrist who will prescribe 'a sense of proportion' and separation from his wife — and precipitate Septimus's death six hours from now.

name: Harley Street — the clocks
quoteAnchor: the clocks of Harley Street nibbled at the June day
passage: Shredding and slicing, dividing and subdividing, the clocks of Harley Street nibbled at the June day, counselled submission, upheld authority, and pointed out in chorus the supreme advantages of a sense of proportion.
note: The cruellest sentence in the book, and a place-specific one: Harley Street was — and remains — the address of expensive private medicine. Woolf makes its clocks complicit.

name: Westminster — Richard brings roses
quoteAnchor: the clock struck three; and she heard nothing else
passage: For with overpowering directness and dignity the clock struck three; and she heard nothing else; but the door handle slipped round and in came Richard!
note: Her husband comes home with roses, having meant all the way from Mayfair to say he loves her, and finding when he arrives that he cannot say it. He gives her the flowers instead.

name: Bloomsbury — Mrs Filmer's lodgings
certaintyNote: The lodging house is not given an address. The Warren Smiths live in Bloomsbury; sited here as a representative point. This waypoint should not be presented as a real address.
quoteAnchor: flung himself vigorously, violently down on to Mrs. Filmer's area railings
passage: He did not want to die. Life was good. The sun hot… Holmes was at the door. "I'll give it you!" he cried, and flung himself vigorously, violently down on to Mrs. Filmer's area railings.
note: Six o'clock. Septimus kills himself rather than be taken away by Dr Holmes. The line that undoes you is 'He did not want to die.' The clock strikes on regardless — one, two, three, four, five, six.

name: Westminster — the party
quoteAnchor: The young man had killed himself; but she did not pity him
passage: The young man had killed himself; but she did not pity him; with the clock striking the hour, one, two, three, she did not pity him, with all this going on.
note: The party Clarissa has spent the whole day preparing. Sir William Bradshaw arrives late and mentions, as small talk, that a patient of his has killed himself. Clarissa goes alone into a small room and thinks about a man she has never met and never will. The two halves of the novel finally touch — and they touch here, in a drawing room in Westminster, at half past ten at night.
